Patch Set 3:

Rosso Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 3: Verified

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/65487c40-e85a-11e2-9a36-52540035b04c).  Ran 15 tests.  No failures.

Patch Set 3: (1 inline comment)

The commit comment needs a much better description of how this is supposed to be used (can be added to the manual when patch is landed), and a test case that demonstrates how to set the policy for different client NIDs.

I haven't done any kind of code inspection yet.

Patch Set 3: (2 inline comments)

This looks to be mixture of TBF and deadline policy, could you explain why it's implemented this way? And this may lead to server idle on throttling.

Patch Set 3:

BTW could you answer the question raised by Nathan on Jira ticket?

Patch Set 3: (1 inline comment)

Yeah, basically, it is a mixture of TBF and deadline policy. 

It is a TBF algorithm for one NID. RPC rate of one NID is controled by TBF algorithm. But also, all RPCs from all NIDs must be scheduled by the policy, so this policy schedules them according to the deadlines of the NIDs.

If the service performance is high enough to meet all the RPC limits, the policy works like TBF algorithm for each NID. If the service performance is not high enough, none of the NIDs will be starved.

Patch Set 3:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/06f2741c-efbb-11e2-b957-52540035b04c).  Ran 2 tests.  2 tests failed: sanityn, sanity.

Patch Set 3:

Basically I'm fine with the code, but IMHO TBF policy is not tightly coupled with deadline policy, so if we can implement them separately, and also implement fallback mechanism mentioned by Andreas, the architecture will be more flexible, eg. we can couple different policies like TBF and ORR together.

Patch Set 3:

Yeah, I agree with Siyao and Andreas.

If the NRS framework supports muti-layered policies, that would be really helpful for many use cases. For example, TBF only supports NID based limit rules now. But we also want to define rules based on UID or JOBID so that we can set limits to RPC per user or per job. If layered policies is supported, we can define TBF polices of three layers, one for NID one for UID, and the other one for JOBID. It is also possible to implement everything and multiple layers in TBF policy too, but layered policies seems simpler and more flexible.
Uploaded patch set 4.

parents.subject: LU-3527 nodemap: add nodemap kernel module

commit.subject: LU-3558 ptlrpc: Add the NRS TBF policy
commit.message:
LU-3558 ptlrpc: Add the NRS TBF policy

The TBF (Token Bucket Filter) policy schedules and throttles all
types of RPCs for traffic control purposes. It divides RPCs into
different types according to their NIDs or job IDs, and enforces
a RPC rate limit on every type. The handling of a RPC will be delayed
until there are enough tokens for the type. Different types are
scheduled according to their deadlines, so that none of them will be
starving even though the service does not have the ability to satisfy
all the RPC rate requirments of types. The RPCs with the the same
types are queued in a FIFO manner.

Signed-off-by: Li Xi <lixi@ddn.com>
Change-Id: I3f73dfbfb451cc44dfe5e0a575ec7ab5b90ac47e
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/6901
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
Tested-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
